items.title: Trump says Iranian response totally unacceptable
items.summary: United States President, Donald Trump, has called Iran's response to a proposal to end the war totally unacceptable. The US proposal sought to end fighting before starting talks on more contentious issues, including Iran's nuclear program. Iranian state TV says that country's response focused on ending the war on all fronts, including Lebanon, and on the safety of shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, without indicating how or when the vital waterway might re-open.

items.title: INTERVIEW: Health Minister Mark Butler ahead of the Budget
items.summary: There are big changes ahead for the NDIS with some budget cuts and tightened eligibility. Health Minister Mark Butler says the government aims to remove around 160,000 participants by the end of the decade. Children with autism are the largest cohort of NDIS participants, with many set to be moved into the government's new thriving kids program and he says future eligibility will be based on a different set of criteria. He says work is also being done on cultural safety before Indigenous participants are transitioned off the NDIS under the new reform measures. Mr Butler's been talking the SBS Chief Political Correspondent Anna Henderson.

items.title: INTERVIEW: PauseAI concerned about controversial new AI tool
items.summary: It's an organisation that wants the world to hit the collective pause button on the development of artificial intelligence. At least until it's known how AI can be built safely and kept under democratic control. Known as PauseAI, it says it's latest concern is the company Anthropic which has just partially released Claude Mythos, a controversial AI tool capable of hacking into complex computer programs. Chief Executive of PauseAI, Maxime Fournes is speaking with Zoe Brock.

items.title: INTERVIEW: Professor Jessica Rosenberg on encouraging young people to engage on quantum technologies
items.summary: How should Australia be preparing students for new technologies? It’s an issue governments and educators are grappling with across the world. American expert in the field, Professor Jessica Rosenberg from George Mason University, has been developing a school curriculum to encourage more young people to engage on quantum technologies. She’s also keen to see more girls and women in the field. During her visit to Australia she sat down with Chief Political Correspondent Anna Henderson to discuss the quantum task ahead.

items.title: INTERVIEW: President of People with Disability Australia Jeramy Hope on NDIS reforms
items.summary: Cuts are coming to the NDIS, with the federal government flagging 160,000 people are expected to be moved off the scheme by 2030 in a push to reign in spending growth. Health Minister Mark Butler says the reforms are necessary to rein in spending growth, with the scheme on track to cost $70 billion per year by the end of the decade. The disability community has expressed alarm and anxiety amid ongoing uncertainty about how the reforms will work and which participants will be affected. Jeramy Hope is the President of People with Disability Australia, and an NDIS participant himself. He is speaking here with Tee Mitchell.

The study, published by the Migrant Justice Initiative, reveals two-thirds of migrant workers are paid less than they are owed under the Fair Work Act, and international students alone are being underpaid $3.18 billion in wages every year. As a result, oil prices have dropped sharply – though remain above US$100 a barrel – and precious metal and commodity prices have risen. The Australian dollar has also benefited, reaching a four-year high, as risk appetite increases. items.title: Some UK elections will be held today. SBS News visited Wales to gauge the mood of voters
items.summary: Millions of voters across Great Britain are heading to the polls today, with elections for the Welsh and Scottish parliaments as well as dozens of English councils. It’s seen as a key test for British Prime Minister Keir Starmer, whose leadership could be challenged if the results are as bad for his Labour party as polls predict. To gauge the mood of voters, SBS News has been to the town of Port Talbot on the South coast of Wales, an area which has been a Labour stronghold for decades - but could fall to Welsh Nationalists or Nigel Farage’s hard-right Reform UK.

items.title: Rising living costs hitting some older Australians hardest and the Australian dollar at fresh 4 year high
items.summary: Senior Australians on government payments are facing some of the sharpest cost of living increases in the country as soaring electricity prices and rising interest rates put pressure on household budgets. In this episode of the SBS On the Money podcast, Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Patricia Sparrow from COTA Australia to find out more, before Laura Cooper from Nuveen shares her outlook on interest rates after the Reserve Bank’s latest decision. Plus, Luke Laretive from Seneca Financial Solutions breaks down a strong rally on the Australian sharemarket as the ASX200 surged following another record session on Wall Street.

items.title: Roblox recruitment: Why so many kids are falling into extremism
items.summary: Online gaming platforms like Roblox and Minecraft have become increasingly popular tools for extremist groups to recruit young people. With Australia's terror threat level at 'probable', the government has announced a new Counter Terrorism Online Centre to disrupt and divert terrorist networks from radicalising young Australians.

Venice Bienniale going ahead despite resignation of entire jury
items.summary: One of the world's most prestigious contemporary art shows, the Venice Biennale, is just days away from its official opening. The opening comes just days after the show's jury resigned prompting a change to the system for awarding the prestigious Golden Lion awards associated with the show. The jury had earlier protested the participation of Israel and Russia saying they wouldn't consider countries with leaders currently charged with crimes against humanity by the International Criminal Court for the awards.

items.title: Interest rates rise again as markets slip and mortgage pressure builds
items.summary: The Reserve Bank has lifted interest rates for the third time this year, pushing the cash rate to 4.35 per cent, even as other major economies pause. In this episode, PWC Chief Economist Amy Lomas unpacks what’s driving the decision and what it signals for the months ahead. The ASX edged lower, falling 0.2 per cent, with Todd Hoare from LGT Wealth Management breaking down how investors reacted. And with borrowers facing rising costs, Sally Tindall from Canstar explains what the latest rate hike means for mortgage repayments and how to assess whether your loan is still competitive.

items.title: Does the Royal Commission into Antisemitism and Social Cohesion go far enough?

While there is broad community support for a Royal Commission, some argue it should be expanded to include other minority perspectives including Muslim and First Nations Australians' experiences of racism.

items.title: RBA expected to lift rates as Australian dollar nears four year high
items.summary: Australian shares dropped 0.4 per cent ahead of an expected Reserve Bank rate rise, with losses across most sectors led by consumer staples and energy, while petrol prices continued to ease nationwide. Corporate headlines included a recall by A2 Milk in the United States, an ASIC investigation into Accent Group, and NAB’s weaker half-year profit driven by accounting changes despite solid lending growth and a steady dividend. The outlook for borrowers and interest rates is in focus as the Australian dollar lifts above 72 US cents. George Boubouras from K2 Asset Management Ltd joins the podcast to break down the market moves and what to watch next.

items.title: 'Hostage to volatility': Fuel prices tipped to spike again as excise relief runs out
items.summary: Drivers are being warned to expect fuel prices at the bowser to rise in the coming weeks.

items.title: 'Schools that look like prisons': Jewish Australians share stories in antisemitism inquiry
items.summary: The mother of a young girl who witnessed the Bondi attack last year told the inquiry Jewish children are growing up in fear.

items.title: Savings over spending: Chalmers says upcoming budget his 'most responsible yet'
items.summary: Federal Treasurer Jim Chalmers has promised to deliver his most responsible budget yet with savings expected across every portfolio. Defending possible changes to negative gearing and capital gains taxes, the government says this budget is about adapting to the uncertain circumstances facing Australia's economy.

items.title: A tanker hit by projectiles in the Strait of Hormuz | SBS News in Easy English 4 May 2026
items.summary: A daily 5-minute news bulletin for English learners and people with a disability.

items.title: What is hantavirus, the suspected disease behind a deadly cruise ship outbreak?
items.summary: Here's what we know about the suspected outbreak of a rare infection that killed three people on a cruise ship on the Atlantic Ocean.

items.title: Suspected hantavirus outbreak on cruise ship kills three, WHO says
items.summary: The World Health Organization said one of three people who died had a confirmed case of the virus.

items.title: The mental health 'safety gap' causing decade-long delays for vulnerable Australians
items.summary: From referral to consultation, accessing mental health care presents many challenges for some Australians, a new report has found.

items.title: Leagh accidentally saw a photo on her partner's phone. It put her in an impossible position
items.summary: These Australians found themselves caught in moral dilemmas. Here's what they decided.

items.title: 'A worrying pattern': Man arrested after attack on nun in Israel
items.summary: Christians in Israel experienced over 150 incidents of harassment and violence in 2025, according to one inter-religious organisation.

items.title: It's probably already in your fridge — and it could help in cutting risk of Alzheimer's
items.summary: Eating seafood twice a week can be important to protect the brain from different diseases.

items.title: Trump to review Iran's new proposal, warns of renewed US strike
items.summary: The US president has said he "can't imagine" that Iran's latest proposal would be acceptable to him.

items.title: NATO, top Republicans question move as US announces troop withdrawal from Germany
items.summary: The German foreign minister said his country was "prepared" for a reduction in US troops and was discussing it with NATO bodies.

items.title: INTERVIEW: North Aboriginal Justice Agency CEO accuses NT government of long term detention in watchhouses
items.summary: The Northern Territory justice system is under fresh scrutiny amid allegations people are being held in overcrowded cells for long periods of time without adequate healthcare in a Darwin correctional facility. A spokesperson for the Department of Corrections has said in a statement that the Darwin Transitional Custody Centre accommodates prisoners for a maximum of four weeks. They said prisoners undergo intake and medical screening to ensure they don't have any serious health concerns and are well suited to the environment. The spokesperson also said prisoners do have access to phones and can contact family. But this week, the North Australia Aboriginal Justice Agency (NAAJA) accused the government of failing to fully address the issue, saying parts of the Darwin City Watchhouse had been renamed and transferred to the control of the corrections department – while conditions continue to resemble a watchhouse. NAAJA CEO Ben Grimes is being interviewed here by Tee Mitchell.

items.title: Colombians divided over the fate of hippos linked to Pablo Escobar
items.summary: Colombia is confronting a unique problem: what to do with a population of hippopotamuses originally linked to Pablo Escobar. The notorious late drug lord became infamous for importing the creatures and establishing a zoo at one of his properties.

items.title: Australia's student visa crackdown hits record highs — what it means for who gets in
items.summary: Australia's student visa crackdown is reshaping who gets in — and exposing a system driven as much by politics as policy.

items.title: Government set to pull back budget spending as delayed economic shock threatens jobs
items.summary: Economists have warned that Australia's historically strong employment rate could be at risk from global pressures.

items.title: Six Australian Gaza aid flotilla activists released in Greece
items.summary: Three released Australian activists have said they were subjected to violence and mistreated while on board an Israeli vessel.

items.title: Trump rejects Iran's latest proposal to end war
items.summary: The US has said it did not want to exit ⁠its confrontation with ‌Iran early "and then have ‌the problem arise in three more years".

items.title: A $1.8m wine loss — and the growing crisis facing Australia's small producers
items.summary: As natural disasters intensify, small producers are left navigating loss, limited support and an uncertain furture.

items.title: INTERVIEW: SBS speaks to Travis Lovett on his 800km Walk for Truth
items.summary: Kerrupmara Gunditjmara man Travis Lovett is now well into his 800 kilometre walk from Melbourne to Canberra. The former Yoorrook Justice Commissioner is leading the five-week long 'Walk for Truth' to call on Prime Minister Anthony Albanese to establish a National Truth Telling Process. SBS reporter Angelica Waite spoke with Mr Lovett when he was in Seymour, about 150 kilometres into his journey.

items.title: Fires cost this couple their $1.8 million wine dream - and they’re not alone
items.summary: As the Australian wine industry struggles with threats including natural disasters, one winemaking family is determined to rebuild after fires destroyed their entire first vintage.

items.title: The world bought into these viral songs. Their similarities raised suspicions
items.summary: Unofficial fan-made FIFA World Cup anthems are taking off on social media — but are they really what they seem?

items.title: It was a 'sleepy' city — now it's one of Australia's hottest property spots
items.summary: Saba moved to Wagga Wagga for a quieter life. Now she's witnessing a boom reshaping the city and its challenges.

items.title: ASX snaps losing streak as bank CEO says Australia will avoid recession despite property risks emerging
items.summary: The ASX has broken an eight-day losing streak, lifted by mining stocks and easing oil prices, while global tech earnings continue to shape investor sentiment. In this episode of SBS On the Money, Morningstar’s Lochlan Halloway unpacks the day’s market action, as ANZ posts a higher profit and signals confidence Australia will avoid recession. Meanwhile, Coles flags further price pressures across supermarket shelves, and Cotality’s Tim Lawless warns Australia’s housing market may be entering a downturn as interest rates bite and growth slows.

items.title: The fight for Farrer: How a rural by-election could signal a change in Australian politics.
items.summary: The May 9 by-election in the New South Wales electorate of Farrer could have major implications for national politics. With One Nation and an independent candidate leading the race, voters in the formerly safe coalition seat represent a growing trend of dissatisfaction with the major parties.

items.title: We all do it every day — but scientists still don’t know why. That may be changing
items.summary: The findings of this study could provide an early clue into one of the body’s long-standing mysteries.

items.title: Iran warns of "long and painful strikes" as US war deadline fast approaches
items.summary: Iran is threatening "long and painful strikes" on US regional positions if the United States conducts any new attacks on their soil. As a deadline fast approaches for US President Donald Trump to make the case for Congress to extend the war, there is uncertainty around the future of a nearly month-long ceasefire. The crucial global shipping route, the Strait of Hormuz, remains an epicentre for the war's stalemate.

items.title: Calls for calm in Alice Springs | SBS News In Easy English 1 May 2026
items.summary: A daily 5-minute news bulletin for English learners and people with a disability.

items.title: 'A new historic chapter': First direct US-Venezuela flight in seven years touches down
items.summary: "It's going to make life so much easier," a passenger boarding the return flight to Miami said.

items.title: INTERVIEW: Prime Minister Anthony Albanese speaks to SBS about the antisemitism Royal Commission report
items.summary: Prime Minister Anthony Albanese speaks to SBS Chief Political Correspondent Anna Henderson in the wake of the release of the interim report by the Royal Commission on Antisemitism and Social Cohesion.

items.title: Andrie recorded a podcast on military influence — then came an acid attack
items.summary: In Indonesia, an acid attack on a prominent activist has pushed concerns over military power and justice to centre stage.

items.title: 'Not one phone call': Families' plea for Australians detained by Israel, as DFAT assists in Crete
items.summary: The Israel Defense Forces intercepted the Global Sumud Flotilla on Thursday near the Greek island of Crete.

items.title: 'Not one phone call': Families' plea for Australians detained by Israel, as DFAT assists in Crete
items.summary: The Israel Defense Forces intercepted the Global Sumud Flotilla on Thursday near the Greek island of Crete.

items.title: King Charles tickles Trump pink, Iran gets trolled - again and the bionic baggage guy
items.summary: The special relationship is given the kiss of life, Iran’s new deal gets kicked into touch as Trump points to a long-term blockade and will Israel’s opposition bromance last? Plus, would you trust a robo-baggage guy?

items.title: Oil breaches US$120, Big Tech’s AI returns
items.summary: The S&P/ASX 200 has extended its losing streak to an eighth straight session, as oil surged past US$120 a barrel following comments from US President Donald Trump that he would extend the naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz. The effect of rising oil prices is being reflected in company reports with Woolworths Group downgrading its full-year profit outlook, despite reporting a lift in third quarter food sales. For more, Stephanie Youssef spoke with TMS Private Wealth Portfolio Manager Ben Clark.

items.title: The first report into an antisemitism Royal Commission has been published. What does it say?
items.summary: The Royal Commission on Antisemitism and Social Cohesion has tabled its interim report, just two months after the commission was launched. Lead commissioner Virginia Bell says the commission has not identified any security issues requiring urgent or immediate action. But the report makes fourteen initial recommendations, including the implementation of nationally consistent gun laws.

items.title: Family, community mourns as outback search for missing girl ends in tragedy
items.summary: WARNING Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander readers are advised that this story includes the name and image of an Indigenous person who has passed away. THIS STORY MAY BE DISTRESSING FOR SOME LISTENERS. The desperate search for a five-year-old missing in the Northern Territory has ended in tragedy. Police believe they've found the girl's body. Chief Minister Lia Finocchiaro says the entire Northern Territory is mourning the family's loss.

items.title: Chalmers has signalled a negative gearing shake-up ahead of the budget. Here's what might change
items.summary: Treasurer Jim Chalmers said even if speculation is correct, "people shouldn’t expect there to be this huge amount of new revenue".

items.title: SBS News in Easy English 30 April 2026
items.summary: A daily five minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

items.title: 'Inevitable failures': Jewish groups respond to Bondi report, urge people to share experiences
items.summary: The government will enact all 14 recommendations made by the interim report into antisemitism, following the Bondi terror attack.

items.title: New report calls for a nationwide strategy on AI in the workplace
items.summary: A new report argues that Australia lacks a national strategy to regulate the spread of artificial intelligence in the workplace. It has been published as an A-I forum is held in Adelaide to examine that issue. The report from the John Curtin Research Centre argues that AI’s impact on Australian workers won’t be decided by technology, but by political choices.

items.title: This city has become a new investment hotspot, but the picture's not all positive
items.summary: Investors' dream or renters' nightmare? The capital city seeing decade-high house prices, as buyers become more cautious.

items.title: 'Slap in the face to the regime': Exiled female footballers finally have reason to celebrate
items.summary: After a five-year battle for FIFA recognition, refugee members of the Afghan women's football team can represent their team internationally.

items.title: Inflation surges as rate bets rise and markets continue to slide
items.summary: A sharp lift in inflation and a seventh straight fall on the ASX set the tone for this episode of the SBS On the Money podcast, unpacking how global conflict is feeding into Australia’s economy. Official data shows annual inflation expanding to 4.6 per cent in March, driven by a surge in fuel and electricity costs. Harry Murphy Cruise from Oxford Economics breaks down what this means for interest rates and how much further prices could rise, while Damien Boey from Wilson Asset Management explains the market reaction, sector moves and what investors are watching next as US tech earnings come into focus.

items.title: Does another news ban loom? What a potential tech levy could mean for you
items.summary: Tech giants have rejected the government's proposed draft law, calling it a "government-mandated transfer of wealth" and "arbitrary".

items.summary: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

items.title: Inflation nears three-year high: What could this mean for Australians?
items.summary: The RBA is expected to raise interest rates for a third time this year following the release of the first economic data since the war began.

items.title: Young Australians may keep pace with their parents — but a deeper wealth rift is emerging
items.summary: Young workers could end up less financially secure than their parents, as inheritance transfers may increase inequality.

items.title: Middle East peace proposal in deadlock as Trump, Iran clash over nuclear talks
items.summary: US-Iran peace talks appear to have stalled again, with the US President reportedly unhappy with the latest proposal from Tehran.

items.title: I could buy my own house. Instead, I choose to live with a 102-year-old man
items.summary: Lubosh Hanuska hasn’t paid rent in six years. Passionate about cohousing, he says he never wants to own property on his own again.

items.title: Zelenskyy criticises Israel over grain shipments 'stolen' by Russia
items.summary: Russia says that growing tensions between Ukraine and Israel are theirs to resolve.

items.title: Diverging markets: why US and Australian shares are going in opposite directions
items.summary: Australian shares extended their losing streak, with the ASX200 down 0.6% to a three-week low and ten of eleven sectors finishing in the red, even as US markets hit fresh records. Utilities led the decline, with Origin Energy tumbling, while a lift in oil prices and a rebound in lithium miners offered some support. For a closer look at the day’s moves and what’s driving the divergence with Wall Street, Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with MPC Markets CEO Mark Gardner.

items.title: Revealed: Here's what the ATO is cracking down on this tax time
items.summary: Bad tax advice could come at a cost, the Australian Taxation Office has warned.

items.title: UK report raises concerns over AUKUS defence pact | SBS News in Easy English 28 April 2026
items.summary: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

items.title: Australians warned to brace for major inflation hit, as Trump's peace plans unclear
items.summary: The US-Israeli war on Iran is threatening to destabilise the global economy.

items.title: Investing for wartime: The online communities looking to make money from conflict
items.summary: While many are feeling economic pain from war in the Middle East, others discuss how to make money.

items.title: Julia Gillard says 'manosphere' requires new approach to gender equality
items.summary: Addressing reporters before the Women Deliver conference, she said "solidarity is not enough."

items.title: ASX hits three week low, as tax time scrutiny ramps up
items.summary: The ASX slips to a three week low ahead of a pivotal week for global markets, with Raymond Chan from Morgans breaking down what to watch as central banks meet, US tech giants report earnings and fresh inflation data lands. At the same time, the countdown to tax time begins, with Assistant Commissioner Anita Challen outlining the ATO’s focus on work related deductions, side hustles and misleading advice circulating online.

items.title: Iran foreign minister arrives in Russia as US talks stall, Israeli strikes continue
items.summary: Abba Araghchi is expected to meet Russian President Vladimir Putin. Planned Iran-US talks in Pakistan collapsed at the weekend.

items.title: Indigenous leaders condemn Anzac Day jeering as debate over Welcome to Country reignites
items.summary: Opposition leader Angus Taylor has condemned Anzac Day heckling but questioned the frequency of Welcome to Country ceremonies.

items.title: Where did Australia rank as global military spending hit a record $4 trillion?
items.summary: The United States, China and Russia accounted for more than half of global defence expenditure, new research reveals.

items.title: Penny Wong warns IS-linked families face 'full force of the law' if they return to Australia
items.summary: The foreign minister reiterated that her government will not assist their return as she prepares to tour Asia to shore up energy supplies.

items.title: Gaza just held its first vote in 20 years. What did it reveal?
items.summary: Gaza's first election in two decades saw low turnout in Deir al-Balah, as Fatah-backed candidates secured most seats.

items.title: Police investigate child abduction in Alice Springs | SBS News in Easy English 27 April 2026
items.summary: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

items.title: Sabastian Sawe makes history by breaking two-hour marathon barrier in London
items.summary: Kenya's Sabastian Sawe has become the first person to shatter the fabled two-hour barrier in the marathon with a breathtaking win in London.

items.title: INTERVIEW: Professor Margie Danchin on falling vaccination rates
items.summary: A paediatrician says misinformation about childhood vaccines coming out of the United States has led to a huge spike in vaccine hesitancy in Australia in the past year. Professor Margie Danchin from the Murdoch Children's Research Institute says the latest figures show Australia has the lowest vaccine coverage for children under 5 in more than a decade, with the rates falling each year since 2020. She spoke with Greg Dyett.

items.title: Australia's 'forgotten' battalion: The men who served without citizenship
items.summary: The Torres Strait Islands is the birthplace of the only all-Indigenous battalion formed in Australia's military history.

items.title: 'Serious consequences': Universities given fresh edict on Iran, Russia and North Korea
items.summary: One university said its warning to staff came after it had received "updated advice" from the federal government.

items.title: Sandra will never own a home. Meet the boomers struggling in the housing crisis
items.summary: While baby boomers still dominate property ownership in Australia, the rate has gone down. Some have been forced to find alternatives.

items.title: Palestinian elections give some in Gaza first chance to vote in two decades
items.summary: Palestinians voted in local elections, the first in two decades in Gaza and the first in the occupied West Bank since the start of the Israel-Hamas war.

items.title: 'Talking about nothing': Peace talks in limbo as Trump cancels envoys' Pakistan trip
items.summary: Trump said he called off the planned visit by his envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner because the talks in Islamabad involved too much travel and expense.

items.title: Are migrant communities and non-citizen taxpayers being forgotten in cost of living conversations?
items.summary: The war in the Middle East has impacted the cost of living in Australia, affecting communities across the country. Representatives for migrant and refugee communities are concerned needs are not adequately being addressed however, as economic and mental health impacts combine.

items.title: 'Carrying things that aren't mine': Anxiety in the age of constant crisis
items.summary: Constant exposure to global crises is fuelling anxiety and reshaping how people stay informed and when they step back.

items.title: Sergio suspected his wife had been unfaithful. So he turned to 'love detectives'
items.summary: In Peru, divorce is on the rise and infidelity can impact the division of assets. Private detectives like Liz play an important role.

items.title: Indigenous leaders, politicians and military chiefs condemn Anzac Day booing
items.summary: Booing disrupted Welcome to Country addresses at Anzac Day dawn services in Melbourne, Perth and Sydney where a man was arrested.

items.title: Meet the military veteran creating comfort and connection through chai
items.summary: A veteran brews more than chai. He's stirring connection, culture and community one sip at a time.

items.title: Fighting for a country that denied their rights: Remembering the service of Australia's Indigenous veterans
items.summary: [[WARNING: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people should exercise caution when listening to this audio as it contains names and images of deceased persons, which may cause distress to members of these communities.]] Since the First World War, Indigenous men and women have served in Australia's defence forces, despite the government's refusal to recognise their rights. NITV Radio's Kerri-Lee Barry has spoken to Michael Bell about members of her own family who served in Australia's defence forces.

items.title: ‘Tea with heart’ – meet the military veteran giving back to others who serve
items.summary: A chain of specialty tea bars founded by a military veteran is expanding rapidly. Ambi's Chai serves tea blended with exotic spices - and its founder is proud his cafes are bringing communities closer.
